cards.heading: Himalayas — Structure and Key Facts
points: Formed by: Collision of Indian Plate (northward moving) with Eurasian Plate; Tethys Sea sediments folded upward; youngest fold mountains in the world; still rising (active orogeny)
Length: 2,500 km from Indus gorge (west) to Brahmaputra gorge (east); width 150–400 km; covers 5 lakh sq km
Three parallel ranges (west to east and north to south):

1. Himadri (Greater Himalaya/Inner Himalaya): Highest peaks; perennially snow-covered; average elevation 6,000m; Mt. Everest (8,849m — Nepal), Kanchenjunga (8,586m — Sikkim-Nepal; highest peak in India), K2/Godwin Austen (8,611m — POK; 2nd highest in world)
2. Himachal (Lesser Himalaya/Middle Himalaya): 3,700–4,500m; Mussoorie, Shimla, Nainital, Ranikhet; Pir Panjal range; Dhauladhar range; Valley of Kashmir here
3. Shiwaliks (Outer Himalaya/Sub-Himalaya): 900–1,100m; FOOTHILLS; Doons/Duars (longitudinal valleys between Shiwaliks and Lesser Himalaya) — Dehra Dun, Patli Dun, Kota Dun
Trans-Himalaya (beyond main range): Karakoram range (K2); Ladakh range; Zanskar range; these are older and harder rocks

Himalayan passes (important): Karakoram Pass (Ladakh — highest motorable pass in world — DISPUTED; Khardung La also claims this); Rohtang Pass (Himachal Pradesh); Zoji La (J&K; connects Srinagar with Ladakh); Nathu La (Sikkim; India-China trade); Bom Di La (Arunachal Pradesh)
Western extensions: Hindukush and Sulaiman ranges (Pakistan/Afghanistan); Arakan Yoma (Myanmar) = eastern extension of Himalaya
cards.alert: ⭐ Kanchenjunga (8,586m) = HIGHEST PEAK in India; K2 (8,611m) = in Pakistan-occupied Kashmir — so sometimes excluded from 'India's peaks'. Mt. Everest = Nepal (not India). Himalayas are FOLD mountains (not volcanic). Indus-Ganga Plain = formed by alluvium deposited by Himalayan rivers = most fertile region. Himalayas act as climate barrier — blocking cold Central Asian winds and forcing monsoon to rain.

rows: Dal Lake; Jhelum river valley; Karewa soils (saffron cultivation); highest density of glaciers; Wular lake (largest freshwater lake in India)
Shimla; apple orchards; Kullu-Manali; Spiti Valley; Rohtang Pass; Corbett National Park (first NP in India 1936); Gangotri glacier
Darjeeling tea (India's 1st GI tag); Kanchenjunga NP; Terai plains at foothills
Kanchenjunga (highest in India); Zemu glacier; only UNESCO Mixed WHS in India (Khangchendzonga NP)
Bom Di La pass; McMahon Line; Brahmaputra gorges; Namdapha NP
Shillong Plateau — detached from main Peninsular Plateau; Cherrapunji+Mawsynram (wettest places on Earth); Garo, Khasi, Jaintia Hills

cards.heading: Peninsular Plateau — Complete Coverage
points: Oldest and most stable landmass of India; part of ancient Gondwana land; composed of old crystalline igneous and metamorphic rocks
Two parts: Central Highlands (north of Narmada river) + Deccan Plateau (south of Narmada river)

Central Highlands: Aravalli range (oldest fold mountains in India; runs NE-SW from Delhi to Gujarat; Guru Shikhar 1722m = highest peak in Aravallis and in Rajasthan); Vindhya range; Satpura range; Chota Nagpur Plateau
Chota Nagpur Plateau (Jharkhand): Rich mineral belt — iron ore (Singhbhum), coal (Jharia, Bokaro, Raniganj), copper, mica; Damodar river valley; Jharia = largest coalfield in India

Deccan Plateau: Triangular; bounded by Western Ghats (west), Eastern Ghats (east), Satpura+Vindhya (north); tilts slightly east (rivers flow east); composed of basalt in northwestern part (Deccan Traps)

Deccan Traps: Northwestern Deccan (Maharashtra, Gujarat, MP, AP); formed by volcanic lava flows 65 million years ago (Cretaceous period — same time as dinosaur extinction); covers 5 lakh sq km; lava created BLACK COTTON SOIL (regur) = fertile for cotton

Western Ghats (Sahyadri): Runs along west coast; continuous; average 1000–1500m; highest = Anamudi (2695m) in Kerala = highest peak in South India; UNESCO WHS (biodiversity hotspot); source of peninsular rivers flowing east; rainfall on western side = high; eastern side (rain shadow) = less rainfall
Eastern Ghats: Discontinuous; broken by rivers (Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Cauvery); average 600m; Mahendragiri (1501m in Odisha = highest in Eastern Ghats)

rows: Oldest fold mountains in India; Delhi Ridge is northern extension
N-S boundary of Deccan Plateau; Narmada flows along its southern edge
Block mountains; Pachmarhi = only hill station in MP; Project Tiger reserve
UNESCO WHS; biodiversity hotspot; highest rainfall; source of Kaveri, Krishna, Godavari
Discontinuous; broken by rivers; much lower than Western Ghats
Joins Western and Eastern Ghats; Ooty (Udhagamandalam) hill station
Southernmost; Agasthyamalai Biosphere Reserve; cardamom cultivation

cards.heading: Northern Plains (Indo-Gangetic Plains)
points: Formation: Alluvial deposits of Himalayan rivers (Indus, Ganga, Brahmaputra and their tributaries) over millions of years in the geosyncline between Himalayas and Peninsular Plateau
Area: 7 lakh sq km; length 2,400 km (Indus to Brahmaputra); width 150–300 km
Most fertile region of India; most densely populated; major agricultural zone
Three sections: Punjab-Haryana Plain (Indus basin; level; wheat+rice); Ganga Plain (main; Bihar to UP; most fertile; rice+wheat+sugarcane); Brahmaputra Plain (assam; floods; jute+rice+tea)

Types of alluvial soil in Northern Plains: Bhangar (older alluvium; higher elevation; less fertile; kankar/calcium carbonate nodules); Khadar (newer alluvium; lower elevation; flood plains; more fertile; renewed every year by floods)
Terai: Belt of wet and marshy land along foothills of Himalayas; thick forests; swamps; cleared for cultivation now; good for sugarcane, rice, jute
Bhabar: Coarse gravel belt immediately below Himalayas (south of Shiwaliks); streams disappear underground here; good for orchards

cards.heading: Thar Desert and Rajasthan
points: Location: Northwestern India; mainly Rajasthan (extends to Gujarat, Punjab, Haryana); also in Pakistan (Sind desert)
Area: 2 lakh sq km in India
Sand dunes: Barchans (crescent-shaped; most common); Seif dunes (longitudinal); Parabolic dunes; dunes cover only 1/5 of desert
Lowest rainfall: Jaisalmer receives 8–10 cm/year; some areas even less

Rivers: Luni river (only major river — drains into Rann of Kutch; saline in lower course); Ghaggar river (seasonal — 'river of lost identity'; considered ancient Saraswati by some scholars; disappears in sands of Rajasthan)
Salt lakes: Sambhar (largest inland salt lake in India — Rajasthan); Didwana; Degana (all in Rajasthan)
Fauna: Great Indian Bustard (critically endangered; Rajasthan + Gujarat); blackbuck; chinkara gazelle; desert fox

Aravalli as barrier: Aravalli blocks moisture-carrying Arabian Sea monsoon from reaching Rajasthan — hence desert; cities on eastern side of Aravalli (Jaipur) get more rain than cities on western side (Jodhpur)

rows: Submerged coast; smooth; few beaches; natural harbours
Emergent coast; wide beaches; sandy; deltaic plains
Rivers short; do not form deltas (fall steeply into sea); backwaters (lagoons/kayals)
Rivers long; form deltas (Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Cauvery deltas)
Vembanad Lake (Kerala; largest in India; 96 km long); Chilika Lake... wait — on E coast
Chilika Lake (Odisha; largest lagoon in India; largest wintering ground for migratory birds in Asia; Irrawaddy dolphin)
Utkal Coast (Odisha); Northern Circars (AP); Coromandel Coast (TN)
Very high (1500–3000mm+); SW monsoon hits first
Mumbai (largest; natural harbour), Kandla, Goa, Mangalore, Kochi (deepest natural harbour)
Chennai, Kolkata (on Hooghly — not on sea), Paradip, Visakhapatnam, Ennore/Kamarajar

cards.heading: Indian Islands
points: Andaman and Nicobar Islands (Bay of Bengal): 572 islands; only 37 inhabited; capital Port Blair; largest island = South Andaman; highest point = Saddle Peak (North Andaman, 738m); only active volcano in India = Barren Island; India's only mud volcano = Baratang Island; Cellular Jail (Kala Pani) at Port Blair; close to equator (Indira Point = southernmost point of India at 6°45'N, in Great Nicobar Island)

Lakshadweep Islands (Arabian Sea): 36 islands; only 10 inhabited; capital Kavaratti; entirely CORAL ISLANDS; smallest UT of India by area and population; no rivers; no forests; coconut trees; only Muslim population; nearest to Kerala; Minicoy (southernmost; closest to Maldives); pony car not used — only walking + boats
Coral reefs in India: Andaman and Nicobar (richest coral reefs); Lakshadweep; Gulf of Mannar (between India and Sri Lanka); Gulf of Kutch
Pamban Island (Rameswaram, Tamil Nadu): India's largest island (not counting A&N and Lakshadweep); connected by Pamban Bridge (first sea bridge in India 1914) + new cable-stayed bridge (2024)
cards.alert: ⭐ Barren Island = ONLY ACTIVE VOLCANO in India (Andaman Islands). Narcondam = dormant volcano (Andaman Islands). Indira Point (Great Nicobar) = southernmost point of India (submerged partially by 2004 tsunami). Chilika Lake (Odisha) = largest coastal lake in India + largest wintering ground for migratory birds in Asia + Irrawaddy dolphins. Vembanad Lake (Kerala) = longest lake in India (96 km).

cards.heading: Monsoon Mechanism — Complete
points: Definition: Seasonal reversal of wind direction; from Arabic 'mausim' (season)

SW Monsoon (June–September): Main rainy season; winds blow from sea to land (Arabian Sea + Bay of Bengal → India); caused by differential heating (land heats faster than sea → low pressure over India → high pressure over Indian Ocean → winds rush inland)

Two branches of SW Monsoon: Arabian Sea Branch (hits Kerala coast first around 1 June; more rainfall; reaches Mumbai, Western Ghats, Gujarat, Rajasthan); Bay of Bengal Branch (hits northeastern India first; moves westward; reaches UP, MP, Bihar)

NE Monsoon (October–December): Winds blow from land to sea; dry winds (already passed over land); BUT pick up moisture over Bay of Bengal → rain to southeastern coast (Tamil Nadu, coastal AP) = Tamil Nadu gets rain in Oct-Dec when rest of India is dry
Break in Monsoon: Temporary pause of 1–2 weeks during SW monsoon; anticyclone develops over NW India; southern India continues to receive rainfall while northern India faces a dry spell
El Nino: Warming of central and eastern tropical Pacific Ocean; weakens SW monsoon over India; associated with droughts in India; La Nina (cooling of Pacific) = often stronger than normal monsoon
Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD): Difference in sea surface temperature between western and eastern Indian Ocean; positive IOD = stronger monsoon; discovered 1999; linked to Indian monsoon rainfall
cards.alert: ⭐ Onset of SW Monsoon: Kerala on 1 June (average); reaches Delhi by 15 July; covers entire India by 15 July approximately. Withdrawal: From northwest (Rajasthan) by 1 September; complete withdrawal by 1 December from southern India. Tamil Nadu = only coastal state that BENEFITS from NE monsoon (when rest of India has post-monsoon dry season).

cards.heading: Four Seasons and Their Characteristics

rows: NE trade winds blow from land to sea
Cold in north; mild in south; western disturbances bring rain to Punjab/Himachal/J&K (important for rabi crops); foggy mornings in IGP; cold wave and frost in north
Local hot winds — 'Loo' in NW; 'Kalbaisakhi' (Nor'westers) in West Bengal
Temperatures 45°C+ in Rajasthan; Loo = hot, dry, dusty wind; Kalbaisakhi = violent thunderstorms (beneficial for tea in Assam, jute in WB); Mango showers in Karnataka+Kerala
SW winds from Arabian Sea + Bay of Bengal
Most rainfall; sudden onset ('burst of monsoon'); Kerala gets rain first; Western Ghats get maximum; Rajasthan least; highest at Cherrapunji/Mawsynram
Winds reverse; blow from land to sea (NE direction)
Cyclones in Bay of Bengal (hit Andhra Pradesh + Tamil Nadu + Odisha); clear sky in most of India; Tamil Nadu gets rain from NE Monsoon

cards.alert: ⭐ Western Disturbances: Extra-tropical cyclones born in Mediterranean Sea; travel east; bring rain/snow to Punjab, Haryana, HP, J&K, Uttarakhand in winter (important for wheat — rabi crop). 'Mango showers' (Kerala + Karnataka in April-May) = pre-monsoon showers that help mango ripening. 'Cherry Blossom showers' in coffee plantations of Karnataka = same phenomenon.

cards.heading: Rainfall Distribution Across India
points: Highest rainfall: Cherrapunji/Sohra (Meghalaya) — average 11,000 mm/year; Mawsynram (Meghalaya) — average 11,872 mm/year (highest in the world); both in rain shadow of Shillong Plateau
Lowest rainfall: Jaisalmer (Rajasthan) — about 8 cm/year; Leh (Ladakh) — cold desert
VERY HIGH rainfall (above 200 cm): Western Ghats slopes, Meghalaya and Assam (NE India), coastal Karnataka, West Bengal (sub-Himalayan), Andaman and Nicobar Islands
HIGH rainfall (100–200 cm): Eastern Rajasthan, eastern UP, Bihar, eastern MP, coastal Tamil Nadu (NE monsoon), most of Odisha, West Bengal plains
MODERATE rainfall (50–100 cm): Most of Rajasthan (except west), western UP, western MP, Deccan Plateau, Tamil Nadu interior
LOW rainfall (less than 50 cm): Rajasthan desert (Thar), Gujarat interior, Deccan rain shadow area (Pune, parts of Karnataka and AP), J&K rain shadow (Leh-Ladakh)
Rain shadow effect: Western Ghats block Arabian Sea branch of SW monsoon — western slope gets very heavy rain (Mahabaleshwar = 600 cm); eastern side (Pune, Sholapur) gets much less (30–50 cm)

cards.heading: Cyclones and Major Climate Zones
points: Tropical Cyclones: Develop over warm tropical oceans (sea surface temperature >26°C); called Typhoons (Pacific), Hurricanes (Atlantic), Cyclones (Indian Ocean); anticlockwise rotation (Northern Hemisphere)

Bay of Bengal cyclones: More frequent and more intense than Arabian Sea cyclones; warmer waters; landfall mostly on Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, West Bengal coasts; October-November = peak season
Arabian Sea cyclones: Less frequent; increasing recently (linked to global warming); landfall on Gujarat, Maharashtra, Kerala coasts; Cyclone Ockhi (2017), Cyclone Tauktae (2021) = recent examples
IMD cyclone scale: Low Pressure → Depression → Deep Depression → Cyclonic Storm → Severe Cyclonic Storm → Very Severe Cyclonic Storm → Extremely Severe → Super Cyclonic Storm
Devastating cyclones: 1999 Odisha Super Cyclone (15,000 deaths); 2020 Amphan (West Bengal); 2021 Yaas (West Bengal+Odisha); 2021 Tauktae (Gujarat+Maharashtra)

rows: Distinct wet (June-Sept) and dry seasons; high temp throughout
Drier than monsoon zone; distinct dry season; grasslands
Less rainfall; extreme temperatures; scrub vegetation
Very low rainfall (<25 cm); extreme heat; sand dunes
Very low rainfall; extreme cold; snow; cold desert
Very high rainfall; high humidity; tropical rainforest
Mild climate; dry summer + wet winter (Western Disturbances)

topics.name: Peninsular Rivers — East and West Flowing

cards.heading: Indus, Ganga and Brahmaputra River Systems

rows: Zaskar, Nubra, Shyok, Hunza (in J&K); Jhelum, Chenab, Ravi, Beas, Sutlej
Flows through J&K; enters Pakistan near Attock; Indus Water Treaty (1960) — India gets Ravi+Beas+Sutlej; Pakistan gets Indus+Jhelum+Chenab
Flows through Dal Lake; forms Wular Lake; enters Pakistan; Indian side called Jhelum, Pakistan side = Hydaspes (ancient)
Largest of Punjab rivers (by discharge); origin at Chandra Bhaga (Chandrabhaga)
Passes through Dalhousie area; enters Pakistan; Ravi flows past Lahore
Westernmost river entirely in India; Alexander's soldiers refused to cross at Beas
Originates in Tibet; cuts through Himalayas near Shipki La; Bhakra Nangal Dam on Sutlej
Gangotri glacier (Uttarakhand); technically from Bhagirathi + Alaknanda
National River of India; Triveni Sangam at Prayagraj (Ganga+Yamuna+mythical Saraswati); meets Bay of Bengal at Bay of Bengal (Ganga Sagar); Sundarbans mangroves in delta
Largest tributary of Ganga; highly polluted in Delhi stretch; joins Ganga at Prayagraj
Subansiri, Jia Bhareli, Dhansiri, Manas, Teesta, Lohit

Longest river in the world by some measures in Tibet; enters India near Pasighat (Arunachal); world's longest river gorge at Namcha Barwa; called Tsangpo (Tibet), Siang (Arunachal), Brahmaputra (Assam), Jamuna (Bangladesh); Majuli Island = world's largest river island

cards.alert: ⭐ Ganga becomes Padma in Bangladesh; Brahmaputra becomes Jamuna. They merge to form Meghna which meets Bay of Bengal. Teesta river = in Sikkim+WB → joins Brahmaputra in Bangladesh. Indus Water Treaty (1960): PM Nehru + President Ayub Khan; World Bank mediated; governs water sharing of 6 Punjab rivers between India and Pakistan.

notes.topic_name: Peninsular Rivers — East and West Flowing

cards.heading: Peninsular Rivers

rows: Hirakud Dam (longest earthen dam in world — 4.8 km); Chilika Lake in delta; Mahanadi delta most productive delta
Longest peninsular river; 'Vriddha Ganga' (Old Ganga/Ganga of South); Papikondalu gorge in AP
Nagarjuna Sagar Dam (largest masonry dam); Tungabhadra tributary (Hampi is on Tungabhadra)

'Dakshina Ganga' (Ganga of South); Ranganathittu + Ranganathaswamy island in river; Shivasamudram waterfall; Mettur Dam (oldest large dam in South India); INTER-STATE WATER DISPUTE (Karnataka vs Tamil Nadu — Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal)

Only major west-flowing peninsular river with a rift valley origin (flows in a trough between Vindhya and Satpura ranges); Marble Rocks at Jabalpur (Bhedaghat); Dhuandhar Falls; Sardar Sarovar Dam (Gujarat) — world's 2nd largest concrete dam; flows east to west — UNUSUAL for peninsular river
Second major west-flowing peninsular river; parallel to Narmada in rift valley; Surat on Tapti
Only major river in Rajasthan desert; saline in lower course; seasonal; drains Thar Desert
Union of Tunga + Bhadra rivers (Western Ghats, Karnataka)
Hampi (Vijayanagara capital) is on Tungabhadra; Tungabhadra Dam (Hospet)
Idukki Dam on Periyar; Periyar Wildlife Sanctuary; important source for Kerala's irrigation

cards.alert: ⭐ East-flowing peninsular rivers form DELTAS (Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Cauvery). West-flowing peninsular rivers (Narmada, Tapti) do NOT form deltas — they flow fast through gorges and the coarse material doesn't settle. Godavari = longest peninsular river. Cauvery = most litigated river (water dispute). Narmada = only major west-flowing peninsular river in a rift valley.

cards.heading: Major Lakes — All for Exam

rows: Fed by Jhelum river; 130–260 sq km; migratory birds
Brackish water lagoon (largest coastal lagoon in India; largest in Asia)
Irrawaddy dolphins; RAMSAR site; largest wintering ground for migratory birds in Asia; Nalabana Island Bird Sanctuary
Saline (largest inland salt lake in India)
Source of salt for India; RAMSAR site; flamingos in winter; 230 sq km
Brackish water (longest lake in India; largest in Kerala)
96 km long; Nehru Trophy Boat Race on Punnamada stretch; Kumarakom Bird Sanctuary on banks; RAMSAR site
Srinagar; houseboats; Hazratbal mosque nearby; surrounded by mountains; shrinking due to encroachment
Freshwater (largest freshwater lake in NE India)
Famous for 'phumdis' (floating islands of vegetation); Keibul Lamjao NP on phumdis (only floating national park in world); Sangai deer (brow-antlered deer) = state animal of Manipur
70 km long; 60% in China; changes color 5 times a day; filming location of 3 Idiots; altitude 4,350m
Biosphere Reserve; breeding ground of bar-headed goose and black-necked crane
Only Brahma temple in world at Pushkar; Pushkar Camel Fair; Aravalli origin
Hyderabad; built by Ibrahim Quli Qutb Shah (1562); Rock of Gibraltar statue in middle
Saltwater crater lake (meteorite impact — ONLY such lake in Asia)
Created by meteorite impact 52,000 years ago; Wildlife Sanctuary; UNESCO tentative list

cards.alert: ⭐ RAMSAR Sites (Wetlands of International Importance) in India: 75+ RAMSAR sites (as of 2023) — most in the world. Largest RAMSAR = Sundarbans (WB). Chilika, Keoladeo, Loktak, Wular, Harike, Sambhar, Bhoj, Point Calimere, Vembanad-Kol, Kolleru = important RAMSAR sites. India has more RAMSAR sites than any other country.

cards.heading: Major Waterfalls and Dams of India

rows: 253m — highest plunge waterfall in India; 4 falls: Raja, Rani, Rover, Rocket; UNESCO tentative list
310m — 2nd highest; Dudhsagar means 'sea of milk'; falls across Konkan Railway line — scenic
80m — 'Niagara of India'; largest waterfall in Kerala
30m — 'Smoke Cascade'; Marble Rocks at Bhedaghat; Jabalpur
90m — one of the earliest hydroelectric power projects (1902)
Height 226m — highest gravity dam in India; largest reservoir = Gobind Sagar Lake
163m height — 2nd largest concrete dam in world; Narmada Bachao Andolan (Medha Patkar led)
260.5m — tallest dam in India + tallest in Asia; rock-fill dam; Tehri Lake
4,800m length — world's longest earthen dam (some say longest dam in India)
Largest masonry dam in world; 409 islands created in reservoir; Nagarjunakonda Buddhist site submerged

cards.heading: All 8 Soil Types — Key Facts for Exam

rows: Northern Plains; coastal plains; river deltas
Wheat, Rice, Sugarcane, Cotton, Jute, Oilseeds
Most fertile; deposited by rivers; Khadar (new, fertile) + Bhangar (old); rich in potash but poor in nitrogen + phosphorus
Deccan Plateau; Maharashtra, MP, Karnataka, AP; Malwa Plateau
Cotton (best for); Sugarcane, Wheat, Groundnut, Tobacco, Citrus
Formed from Deccan Trap lava; self-ploughing (cracks when dry — mixes itself); holds moisture well; fertile without irrigation; poor in nitrogen+phosphorus+organic
Eastern Plateau (Odisha, Chhattisgarh, AP, TN, parts of MP and UP)
Red color due to iron oxide; yellow when hydrated; poor in nitrogen, phosphorus, organic; porous and less fertile
Kerala, Karnataka, TN, Odisha, Chhattisgarh (high rainfall areas)
Formed by leaching in heavy rainfall; silica leaches out; aluminum+iron remain; hard surface; cannot hold water; acidic; poor fertility
Sandy; poor in organic matter; high in soluble salts; low moisture retention; alkaline; can be productive with irrigation (Indira Gandhi Canal transformed Rajasthan)
Formed by breakdown of rocks; varies greatly with altitude; acidic in nature; thin layer; good for coniferous forests
Kerala backwaters, Sundarbans WB, coastal Odisha, Almora district
High organic matter; water-logged; acidic; rich in humus; very limited distribution
Arid regions of Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana, UP (Usar/Reh/Thur/Bhur)
Excessive salt content; 'reh' or 'usar' land; caused by poor drainage and high evaporation; can be reclaimed

cards.alert: ⭐ Soil distribution: Alluvial = largest area (about 40%); Red = second largest; Black = third (about 15%); Laterite = 4th. Black soil = BEST for cotton = also called 'regur' (from Telugu word). Laterite = 'brick' in Latin; used as building material in Kerala. Alluvial soil classified by ICAR (Indian Council of Agricultural Research) into 8 types. Soil erosion = wind erosion (Rajasthan), water erosion (NE+peninsular rivers), gully erosion (Chambal ravines).

cards.heading: Types of Natural Vegetation in India

rows: >200 cm rainfall; Western Ghats, NE India, Andaman
Teak (best timber), Sal, Sandalwood, Khair, Sheesham
Sal forests = UP, MP, Jharkhand; Teak = Maharashtra, MP, Karnataka
Acacia, Euphoria, Cactus, Date palm, Babool (Khejri = state tree of Rajasthan)
Thar Desert margin; Deccan rain shadow; Gujarat
Lower: Shorea, Sal; Middle: Oak, Chestnut, Rhododendron; Higher: Pine, Fir, Spruce, Cedar (Deodar); Timberline: Alpine meadows (Bugyals)
Deodar = Uttarakhand state tree; Cedar in J&K
Sundari (from which Sundarbans is named), Rhizophora, Avicennia
Sundarbans (WB) = world's largest mangrove; Bhitarkanika (Odisha); Pichavaram (TN); Coringa (AP)

cards.alert: ⭐ Largest forest area: MP (77,000+ sq km) > Arunachal Pradesh > Chhattisgarh > Odisha > Maharashtra. Highest forest cover percentage: Mizoram (85%+) > Meghalaya > Manipur. Teak and Sal = most important commercially. Sandalwood = Karnataka (state tree of Karnataka = Sandalwood tree). Khejri = Rajputs used for fodder + food — Chipko-like movement (Bishnoi community protected khejri trees). Chipko Movement (1973, Uttarakhand): Sundarlal Bahuguna; women hugged trees to prevent felling; 'hug the tree' movement; led to ban on felling in Himalayas.

cards.heading: Coal — Distribution and Types
points: India = 4th largest coal reserves in world; 3rd largest producer

Types: Anthracite (highest carbon 92–98%; hard; best quality; very limited in India — J&K), Bituminous (80–90% carbon; most common; used in industry), Lignite/Brown coal (low carbon 40–55%; soft; Neyveli TN = largest lignite mine), Peat (lowest carbon; not commercial)
Distribution: 80%+ in Gondwana coalfields (Damodar Valley — most important); rest in Tertiary coalfields (NE India, J&K)
Damodar Valley coalfields: Jharia (Jharkhand — LARGEST coalfield in India; coking coal); Raniganj (WB — oldest coalfield in India); Bokaro (Jharkhand); Ramgarh (Jharkhand); Korba (Chhattisgarh)
Other coalfields: Singrauli (MP-UP border); Wardha Valley (Maharashtra); Talcher (Odisha); Godavari Valley (Telangana); Umaria (MP)
Neyveli Lignite: Tamil Nadu; open cast mine; feeds thermal power station; India's largest lignite deposit

rows: Largest oil field in India; offshore; discovered 1974; ONGC operates
First commercial oil well in India after independence
Oldest oil field in Asia (1889); still producing; oldest refinery in Asia
Major oil field in NE India
KG Basin; major gas (Reliance D6 block); Mangamaripeta oil field
Oldest refinery after Digboi; largest public sector refinery
Near Agra; controversy over fumes harming Taj Mahal
NE India; part of NE peace accord

cards.heading: Iron Ore, Steel and Key Minerals

rows: India = 4th largest iron ore reserves; 2nd largest exporter; Hemantite (best quality) and Magnetite are main types
Jharkhand, WB, MP, Chhattisgarh, Odisha, Telangana
4th largest reserves; Jharia = largest coalfield (coking coal)
Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, MP, Gujarat, Maharashtra
India = 5th largest bauxite reserves; Odisha = largest producer
Very low reserves; Khetri = 'Copper City'; major importer
India = world's largest mica producer; used in electrical industry; Jharkhand = Koderma district
Used in steel industry + batteries; India = 4th largest producer
Karnataka (Kolar Gold Fields — KGF; deepest gold mine in Asia; now closed), Andhra Pradesh
KGF mines now government owned; Karnataka = major producer
India = world's largest thorium reserves; important for nuclear power
Jaduguda = first uranium mine in India (1967)
India = 3rd largest chromite producer; Sukinda = world's largest open cast chromite mine
Used in cement industry; India = major cement producer
Only diamond mines in India; limited production

cards.heading: Conventional and Non-conventional Energy Sources
points: Thermal Power: Coal-based thermal plants generate most electricity in India (~60%); major plants at Vindhyachal (MP), Singrauli (MP-UP border), Korba (Chhattisgarh), Ramagundam (Telangana), Rihand (UP), Talcher (Odisha)

Hydroelectric Power: About 13% of installed capacity; major: Bhakra-Nangal (Sutlej), Bhilai+Damodar Valley, Nagarjuna Sagar (Krishna), Koyna (Maharashtra — largest HEP in Maharashtra), Salal (J&K), Dulhasti (J&K), Tehri (Uttarakhand)

Nuclear Power: About 3% of total; plants at Tarapur (Maharashtra — first nuclear plant 1969), Rawatbhata (Rajasthan), Kalpakkam (Tamil Nadu — first Fast Breeder Reactor under construction), Narora (UP), Kakrapar (Gujarat), Kaiga (Karnataka), Kudankulam (TN — Russian built; largest nuclear plant)

Solar Power: India has vast potential; Rajasthan, Gujarat best potential; major parks: Bhadla Solar Park (Rajasthan — world's largest solar park, 2245 MW), Kamuthi Solar Power Plant (TN — was world's largest single-location solar plant briefly), Gujarat Solar Park

Wind Power: India = 4th largest wind power producer; Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Rajasthan, AP have best potential; Muppandal Wind Farm (TN) = largest wind farm in India; Jaisalmer Wind Park (Rajasthan)
Target: India aims for 500 GW renewable energy by 2030 (announced at COP26)

cards.heading: Three Crop Seasons — Kharif, Rabi, Zaid

rows: Rice, Jowar (Sorghum), Bajra, Maize, Cotton, Groundnut, Jute, Sugarcane, Soyabean, Tur (Arhar dal), Urad, Moong
Rice: WB, UP, Punjab; Cotton: Maharashtra, Gujarat; Groundnut: Gujarat, AP; Jute: WB
Wheat, Barley, Gram (Chickpea), Mustard, Linseed, Peas, Masur (Lentil)
Wheat: Punjab, Haryana, UP, MP, Rajasthan; Gram: MP, Maharashtra; Mustard: Rajasthan
Watermelon, Muskmelon, Cucumber, Vegetables, Fodder crops
UP (Ganga-Yamuna Doab), Punjab, Haryana; requires intensive irrigation
Tea, Coffee, Rubber, Coconut, Spices, Tobacco
Tea: Assam+WB; Coffee: Karnataka; Rubber: Kerala
India = 2nd largest producer in world; staple food of eastern+southern India
India = 2nd largest producer; mainly N.India; Green Revolution success crop
India = 2nd largest producer + largest consumer; UP = largest producer; Maharashtra = highest productivity
India = 3rd largest producer; Black soil = best for cotton; Bt cotton (GMO) = most widely grown
India = 2nd largest producer (after China); Assam = 52% of India's tea; Darjeeling tea = GI tag
Arabica and Robusta varieties; Bababudan Hills (Chikmagalur Karnataka) = origin of coffee cultivation in India; brought by Baba Budan (Sufi saint) from Yemen
India = 5th largest natural rubber producer; Kerala plantation sector
India = 2nd largest producer after China; edible oil + cattle feed
India = largest spice exporter in world; 'Spice Bowl of World'

cards.heading: Types of Irrigation and Major Projects
points: Canal Irrigation: Most important; mainly in IGP (UP, Punjab, Haryana, Bihar, WB); also Rajasthan Canal / Indira Gandhi Canal (Rajasthan — 649 km main canal; transforms Thar Desert; from Sutlej+Beas rivers at Harike barrage); TN (from Cauvery); Andhra Pradesh (Krishna+Godavari)
Well and Tube Well Irrigation: Most widely used type in India (by area irrigated); mainly IGP; Punjab, Haryana, UP, Bihar; ground water depletion is major concern
Tank Irrigation: Mainly South India (Tamil Nadu, AP, Karnataka); tanks = artificial reservoirs; Chembarambakkam Lake (Chennai's main water source); also Odisha
Drip and Sprinkler Irrigation: Water efficient; promoted by government; Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Karnataka; important for water conservation
Major Irrigation Projects:
Indira Gandhi Canal (Rajasthan): Longest canal in India; from Harike Barrage (Sutlej+Beas); transforms Rajasthan desert
Upper Ganga Canal (UP): From Haridwar; oldest major canal in India
Nagarjuna Sagar (AP/Telangana): On Krishna river; Largest masonry dam; major irrigation for AP and Telangana
Tungabhadra Project (Karnataka/AP): Joint; Tungabhadra river; important for northern Karnataka+AP
Bhakra-Nangal (Punjab/HP): Sutlej river; major irrigation for Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan

cards.heading: Color-Coded Revolutions in Indian Agriculture

rows: Food grains (wheat+rice); HYV seeds; increased production
Milk/Dairy; made India world's largest milk producer
Verghese Kurien ('Milkman of India'); NDDB; Amul model
India = 2nd largest fish producer; major aquaculture growth
India became self-sufficient in edible oils period (then gap again)
Nirpakh Tutaj; major push to fruit cultivation; NE India fruits
India = 3rd largest egg producer in world
Dr. M.S. Swaminathan advocated holistic development
Dr. M.S. Swaminathan's concept for 21st century

cards.alert: ⭐ Green Revolution: Punjab, Haryana, W.UP = main beneficiaries; IR-8 (miracle rice) + Sonora-64/Lerma Rojo (wheat); HYV = High Yielding Variety. Negative effects: Groundwater depletion, soil degradation, pesticide overuse, regional inequality, only rice+wheat (not pulses+millets). Evergreen Revolution = Dr. Swaminathan's concept for sustainable food security. NDDB (National Dairy Development Board) = Verghese Kurien founded 1965 at Anand, Gujarat.

cards.heading: Iron and Steel Plants in India

rows: Private (first in India; oldest integrated steel plant)
USSR collaboration (originally US proposed but they withdrew)
First coastal steel plant in India; 'Steel City of India'
Second largest steel producer in India

cards.alert: ⭐ TISCO = FIRST private sector integrated steel plant in India (1907, Jamshedpur — founded by J.N. Tata; advice by German metallurgist Julius Vogelstein). VIZAG = first coastal steel plant. Bhilai+Durgapur+Rourkela = 1959 = 2nd Five Year Plan steel plants. SAIL (Steel Authority of India Limited) = public sector umbrella; controls Bhilai, Durgapur, Rourkela, Bokaro, IISCO.

rows: Mumbai (was 'Cottonopolis of India'), Ahmedabad (Manchester of India), Coimbatore, Surat, Nagpur, Indore
India = world's largest cotton yarn exporter + 2nd largest textile exporter
Kolkata region (Hooghly River belt) — Rishra, Serampore, Baranagar, Howrah
India = largest jute producer + largest jute goods producer; Bangladesh = largest raw jute exporter
Karnataka (Mysore — mulberry silk; Kolar, Ramanagara), WB (Murshidabad), Tamil Nadu, Assam (Sualkuchi)
India = 2nd largest silk producer (after China); Bangalore = silk city; Sualkuchi = Manchester of the East (Assam)
UP + Maharashtra = 60%+ of India's sugar; also Bihar, Karnataka, TN
India = 2nd largest sugar producer after Brazil; UP = largest producer
India = 2nd largest cement producer in world; ACC (1936) = oldest cement company
Gujarat (largest — Ankleshwar, Vadodara, Surat), Mumbai, Pune, TN
Hyderabad (Pharma capital of India), Mumbai, Ahmedabad, Pune, Bengaluru
India = 3rd largest pharma producer in volume; 'Pharmacy of the World'
Bengaluru (Silicon Valley of India), Hyderabad (Cyberabad), Pune, Chennai, Noida-Gurugram, Kolkata
India = largest IT services exporter; TCS, Infosys, Wipro = top companies
Chennai (Detroit of India), Pune, Gurugram-Manesar, Bengaluru, Kolkata
India = 3rd largest auto market in world; Maruti Suzuki (largest car maker); TVS+Hero (two-wheelers)

cards.heading: Transport Networks of India
points: Indian Railways: Largest rail network in Asia; 4th largest in world; 68,000+ km route length; 7,000+ stations; 1.3 crore passengers daily; first train 16 April 1853 (Bori Bunder-Thane, 34 km); 18 railway zones (HQ: CST Mumbai/Chennai/Kolkata/Delhi/etc.)
Railway gauges: Broad gauge (1.676m — 90% of network); Metre gauge; Narrow gauge; All being converted to Broad gauge (Project Unigauge)
Mountain Railways: Darjeeling Himalayan Railway (2ft gauge — UNESCO WHS); Nilgiri Mountain Railway (Ooty — only rack-and-pinion railway in India — UNESCO WHS); Kalka-Shimla Railway (UNESCO WHS)

National Highway Network: NH-44 = longest NH in India (3,745 km — Srinagar to Kanyakumari); NH-48 = Delhi-Mumbai-Bangalore; NH-27 = East-West corridor; Golden Quadrilateral (Delhi-Mumbai-Chennai-Kolkata = 5,846 km; NHAI project; completed 2012)

Major Ports: 13 major ports; Kandla (Deendayal) = largest cargo port; Jawaharlal Nehru Port Trust (JNPT/Nhava Sheva) = busiest container port; Paradip (Odisha) = on east coast; Chennai = oldest artificial port; Vizag = deepest natural port; Kochi = deepest natural harbour; Kolkata (on Hooghly) = only riverine major port
Inland Waterways: National Waterway-1 = Allahabad to Haldia (Ganga river — 1,620 km; longest); NW-2 = Sadiya to Dhubri (Brahmaputra); NW-3 = West Coast Canal (Kerala — 205 km)

Air Transport: Indira Gandhi International Airport (Delhi) = busiest in South Asia; Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport (Mumbai) = 2nd; Air India = national carrier (privatized to Tata Group 2022)

cards.heading: Census 2011 — Must Know Facts
points: Total population: 121.09 crore (1.21 billion); 2nd largest in world after China (now India is 1st as of 2023 — 143 crore)
Decadal growth rate (2001–2011): 17.7% (lowest since 1951); population growth rate declining
Census 2011 motto: 'Our Census Our Future'
Census Commissioner: C. Chandramauli
Population density: 382 persons/sq km (India average; UP has 828, WB has 1029)
Sex ratio: 943 females per 1000 males (2011); improved from 933 (2001); best ever since 1971
Child sex ratio (0–6 years): 919 girls per 1000 boys — very concerning; declined from 927 (2001)
Literacy rate: 74.04% (2011); Male 82.14%; Female 65.46%; improved from 64.8% (2001)

cards.heading: Population Distribution and Urbanization
points: Northern Plains = most densely populated region (Bihar 1106, WB 1029, UP 828); fertile land + good transport + economic opportunity
NE states, Himalayas, Thar Desert = sparsely populated; difficult terrain/climate
India's demographic dividend: 65%+ population under 35 years (2022); large working-age population = economic opportunity IF properly educated and employed

Population policy: National Population Policy 2000; Total Fertility Rate (TFR) target = 2.1 (replacement level); currently 2.0 (2020) — TFR already at replacement level; South India and Kerala lower than north India
Urbanization: 31.16% urban population (2011); Urban India growing; 53 cities with 10 lakh+ population; Mumbai = most populous city
Million Cities (2011): 53 cities with 10 lakh+ population; 8 cities with 50 lakh+ (Mumbai 1.25 crore, Delhi 1.1 crore, Kolkata 91 lakh, Chennai 70 lakh, Bangalore 84 lakh)
Migration: Internal migration mainly from UP+Bihar+Rajasthan to Maharashtra, Gujarat, Delhi; inter-state migration for employment; push factors (poverty, drought) and pull factors (jobs, amenities)

cards.heading: Literacy, Sex Ratio and Density — State-wise Rankings
cards.alert: ⭐ Kerala = most educated, best sex ratio, highest HDI state. Bihar = lowest literacy, highest density state. Nagaland = only state with NEGATIVE population growth (2001-2011) — due to migration. Child sex ratio most concerning in Haryana (834) despite being prosperous state — sex-selective abortion. Census 2021 (due in 2021) postponed due to COVID — yet to be conducted.

rows: Pacific = larger than all land combined
Debate: Amazon might be longer; Nile = traditionally accepted
Australia counted as continent not island
Andes also longest in world; Himalayas = highest
Everest height revised to 8,849m in 2020 by Nepal+China joint survey (was 8,848m)

cards.heading: Frequently Asked Countries and GK
points: Landlocked countries bordering India: Nepal (capital Kathmandu), Bhutan (capital Thimphu) — both landlocked; Nepal = buffer state between India and China
Smallest country in world: Vatican City (0.44 sq km; Rome, Italy); followed by Monaco; San Marino
Most island-dependent country: Maldives (1192 islands; capital Male; all low-lying — threatened by sea level rise); Tuvalu; Marshall Islands; Kiribati

Straits important for India: Palk Strait (between India and Sri Lanka; very shallow; Ram Setu/Adam's Bridge of islands here); Strait of Malacca (between Malay Peninsula and Sumatra; busiest shipping lane in world; India-China trade route); Strait of Hormuz (Iran-Oman; oil tankers from Middle East pass through here)
International Date Line: 180° meridian; roughly along Pacific Ocean; Samoa, Tonga, Fiji east of it; Japan, Australia, NZ west of it; arbitrary adjustments for island nations
Major Passes in World: Khyber Pass (Pakistan-Afghanistan; Hindu Kush; historic invasion route); Bolan Pass (Pakistan; connects Quetta-Sind); Karakoram Pass (India-China; high altitude)
Deserts of World: Sahara (Africa; hot); Gobi (China-Mongolia; cold); Arabian (Arabia); Atacama (Chile; driest place on Earth; 0.1 mm/year); Patagonian (Argentina; cold); Great Victoria (Australia)

cards.heading: Major World Biomes and Climate Zones

rows: High rainfall (>200 cm), High temperature, High biodiversity; 50% of all species; canopy structure
Seasonal rainfall; distinct dry season; scattered trees; grasses dominant
< 25 cm rainfall; extreme temperature variation; sandy/rocky
Camel, Fennec fox, Roadrunner, Saharan horned viper
Mediterranean basin, California, Chile, SW Australia, SW South Africa
Hot dry summer + mild wet winter; evergreen shrubs; UNIQUE climate
Low rainfall; extreme temperature; no trees; fertile soils
Eastern USA, Western Europe, Eastern China, Japan
Moderate rainfall; distinct seasons; trees shed leaves in autumn
Oak, Maple, Deer, Bear, Fox, Squirrel
Long cold winters; short summers; coniferous trees (pine, fir, spruce, larch)
Wolf, Moose, Lynx, Wolverine, Brown bear
Permafrost; no trees; mosses+lichens; very cold; brief summer
Polar bear, Arctic fox, Musk ox, Reindeer/Caribou
Distinct wet and dry season; SW monsoon; high rainfall in wet season
Indian Tiger, Leopard, Peacock, Indian Elephant

cards.alert: ⭐ Major Biodiversity Hotspots (Norman Myers concept; Conservation International): 36 hotspots globally; in India: Western Ghats + Sri Lanka hotspot; Eastern Himalayas; Indo-Burma; Sundaland (partly covers Andaman). Hotspot criteria: 1500+ endemic plant species AND lost 70%+ of original habitat. India hosts 2 major hotspots fully + 2 partially.
